description: A common viral infection of the upper respiratory tract caused mainly by rhinoviruses. Usually mild and resolves within 7-10 days.
precautions: Wash hands frequently to prevent spreading
whenToSeeDoctor: If fever exceeds 39°C (102°F), symptoms last more than 10 days, difficulty breathing, severe headache or sinus pain.
dietAdvice: Warm fluids, chicken soup, honey with warm water, vitamin C rich foods

description: A contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses. More severe than the common cold with sudden onset of symptoms.
precautions: Stay home and rest for at least 24 hours after fever breaks
Cover mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing
whenToSeeDoctor: Difficulty breathing, chest pain, persistent vomiting, confusion, severe or persistent vomiting, symptoms improve then return with fever.
dietAdvice: Clear broths, electrolyte drinks, soft easily digestible foods

description: A chronic condition where blood pressure in the arteries is persistently elevated. Often called the 'silent killer' as it may have no symptoms.
precautions: Reduce salt intake to less than 5g per day
Manage stress through meditation or yoga
whenToSeeDoctor: BP consistently above 140/90 mmHg, severe headache, vision problems, chest pain, difficulty breathing, or irregular heartbeat.
dietAdvice: DASH diet: fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy, whole grains, lean protein. Avoid high sodium processed foods.

description: A metabolic disorder characterized by high blood glucose levels due to insulin resistance or insufficient insulin production.
precautions: Follow a low glycemic index diet
Exercise for at least 30 minutes daily
Avoid sugary drinks and processed foods
whenToSeeDoctor: Blood sugar consistently above 250 mg/dL, symptoms of hypoglycemia (shakiness, confusion), foot ulcers, vision changes.
dietAdvice: Low GI foods, whole grains, leafy vegetables, lean proteins. Avoid refined sugars, white bread, sugary beverages.

description: A mosquito-borne viral infection causing severe flu-like illness. Can develop into severe dengue (hemorrhagic fever) which can be life-threatening.

medicines.name: Paracetamol
medicines.warning: DO NOT use Aspirin or Ibuprofen — they increase bleeding risk

whenToSeeDoctor: IMMEDIATELY if platelet count falls below 100,000, bleeding from gums or nose, blood in urine/stool, severe abdominal pain, rapid breathing.
dietAdvice: Papaya leaf juice (may help platelets), coconut water, fresh fruits, light digestible foods. Stay well hydrated.

description: A life-threatening disease caused by Plasmodium parasites transmitted through infected mosquito bites. Requires prompt treatment.
precautions: Seek immediate medical care if symptoms appear
whenToSeeDoctor: ALWAYS see a doctor immediately for suspected malaria. Seek emergency care for confusion, seizures, difficulty breathing, or jaundice.
dietAdvice: Light easily digestible foods, plenty of fluids, avoid fatty foods during fever phases.

description: A bacterial infection caused by Salmonella typhi, spread through contaminated food and water. Can be severe without treatment.
precautions: Drink only boiled or bottled water
Wash hands before eating and after toilet
Avoid raw vegetables and street food
whenToSeeDoctor: All suspected typhoid cases need medical attention. Emergency: intestinal perforation symptoms (severe abdominal pain, rigid abdomen), severe bleeding.
dietAdvice: Soft, easily digestible foods: khichdi, curd rice, banana, boiled vegetables. Avoid spicy, oily, high-fiber foods.

description: A chronic inflammatory disease of the airways causing recurrent episodes of wheezing, breathlessness, and coughing, often triggered by allergens or exercise.
precautions: Identify and avoid triggers (dust, pollen, smoke)

medicines.name: Salbutamol (Albuterol) Inhaler
medicines.duration: As needed (not more than 4 times daily)

whenToSeeDoctor: Inhaler not helping within minutes, lips turning blue, difficulty speaking, severe breathlessness at rest.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet: fruits, vegetables, nuts. Avoid sulfites (in wine, dried fruits), aspirin if sensitive.

description: A neurological condition characterized by intense, debilitating headaches often accompanied by sensory disturbances. Can last 4-72 hours.
precautions: Identify and avoid personal triggers (stress, certain foods, bright lights)
Rest in dark, quiet room during attack

medicines.name: Sumatriptan (Imigran)
medicines.frequency: At onset, may repeat after 2 hours

whenToSeeDoctor: Sudden worst headache of life (thunderclap), headache with fever/stiff neck, headache after head injury, progressive worsening over days.
dietAdvice: Avoid triggers: aged cheese, red wine, chocolate, caffeine (or ensure consistent intake). Regular meal times important.

description: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines caused by viral, bacterial, or parasitic infections, often from contaminated food or water.
precautions: Rest the gut with light foods
Avoid dairy, fatty, spicy foods temporarily

medicines.name: Loperamide (Imodium)
medicines.adultDose: 4mg initially then 2mg after each loose stool

whenToSeeDoctor: Blood in stool or vomit, severe dehydration (no urination for 8 hours), fever above 39°C, symptoms lasting more than 3 days, diarrhea in infants.
dietAdvice: BRAT diet: Bananas, Rice, Applesauce, Toast. Gradually return to normal diet. Avoid dairy, caffeine, alcohol.

description: An infectious disease caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus. Most people experience mild to moderate illness; severe disease occurs in older adults and those with underlying health conditions.
precautions: Wear N95 mask if around others
Monitor oxygen levels with pulse oximeter
Ventilate rooms and maintain fresh air
whenToSeeDoctor: Oxygen saturation below 94%, difficulty breathing, persistent chest pain, confusion, bluish lips or face, high fever not responding to medication.
dietAdvice: High protein foods to aid recovery, vitamin C and zinc rich foods, warm fluids, avoid processed foods. Turmeric milk is beneficial.

description: A highly contagious viral infection caused by varicella-zoster virus, characterized by itchy blister rash. Common in children but can be severe in adults.
precautions: Stay home until all blisters have crusted over
Avoid scratching to prevent scarring and infection
Avoid contact with pregnant women, newborns, and immunocompromised individuals

medicines.name: Calamine Lotion
medicines.adultDose: Apply to affected areas 3-4 times daily

whenToSeeDoctor: High fever over 39°C, rash spreading to eyes, severe skin infection from scratching, difficulty breathing, confusion, or stiff neck.
dietAdvice: Soft, non-spicy foods if mouth sores develop. Cold yogurt, ice cream help soothe mouth lesions. Stay well hydrated.

description: A bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily affecting the lungs. A leading infectious disease killer worldwide but treatable with a full course of antibiotics.
precautions: Complete the full 6-month antibiotic course without skipping doses
All close contacts should be tested
Avoid public places during infectious period
whenToSeeDoctor: Any suspicion of TB requires immediate doctor consultation. Do not self-medicate. TB is a notifiable disease requiring supervised treatment.
dietAdvice: High calorie, high protein diet to combat weight loss. Milk, eggs, meat, legumes, nuts. Vitamin B6 supplement recommended with Isoniazid.

description: A bacterial infection in any part of the urinary system. More common in women. Usually affects the bladder (cystitis) but can spread to kidneys if untreated.
precautions: Wipe front to back after using toilet
Avoid holding urine for long periods

medicines.name: Phenazopyridine (AZO)
medicines.duration: Max 2 days (only for symptom relief, not cure)
medicines.warning: Will turn urine orange/red — this is normal

whenToSeeDoctor: High fever with chills, back/flank pain, nausea/vomiting (signs of kidney involvement), pregnant women with any UTI symptoms, recurrent UTIs (3+ per year).
dietAdvice: Drink plenty of water and unsweetened cranberry juice. Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods during infection. Probiotics help restore urinary tract flora.

description: Hard deposits of minerals and salts that form inside kidneys. Can cause severe pain as they pass through the urinary tract. Varies from small (pass naturally) to large (requiring intervention).
precautions: Drink at least 3-4 liters of water daily
Reduce salt and animal protein intake
Avoid vitamin C supplements in excess
Follow up with urologist for recurrent stones
whenToSeeDoctor: Fever with chills (infection + obstruction), unable to control pain, stone larger than 6mm (unlikely to pass naturally), only one functioning kidney.
dietAdvice: High water intake is critical. Lemon water helps (citrate prevents calcium stones). Reduce red meat, salt, and oxalate foods.

description: A chronic autoimmune inflammatory disease that primarily affects joints, causing progressive damage if untreated. Can affect multiple organ systems.
precautions: Regular blood tests to monitor disease activity

medicines.name: Methotrexate
medicines.warning: Take folic acid supplement to reduce side effects. Avoid alcohol.

whenToSeeDoctor: Newly swollen or painful joints, inability to perform daily activities, signs of infection while on immunosuppressants, severe medication side effects.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet: fatty fish (omega-3), olive oil, leafy greens, berries, turmeric. Minimize processed foods, red meat, and alcohol.

description: A condition where the thyroid gland produces insufficient thyroid hormone, slowing down metabolism. Very manageable with daily medication.
precautions: Take thyroid medication on empty stomach at same time daily
Regular TSH blood tests every 6-12 months
Avoid taking with calcium or iron supplements (2-4 hour gap)
Inform all doctors about thyroid condition

medicines.name: Levothyroxine (Eltroxin/Thyronorm)
medicines.frequency: Once daily on empty stomach (30-60 min before breakfast)

whenToSeeDoctor: Symptoms not improving on medication, difficulty swallowing, rapid weight changes, palpitations, symptoms of myxedema (severe hypothyroidism).
dietAdvice: Adequate iodine intake (iodized salt, seafood). Limit goitrogens (raw cabbage, broccoli) in large amounts. Selenium-rich foods like Brazil nuts.

description: The most common type of anemia, caused by insufficient iron to produce hemoglobin. Very common in women of reproductive age, pregnant women, and vegetarians.
precautions: Never take iron with tea, coffee, or dairy (reduces absorption)
Take iron with vitamin C to enhance absorption
Check for underlying causes (menstrual blood loss, GI bleeding)

medicines.name: Ferrous Sulfate
medicines.childDose: 3-6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron in divided doses
medicines.frequency: 2-3 times daily between meals or with Vitamin C

whenToSeeDoctor: Hemoglobin below 8 g/dL, symptoms not improving after 4 weeks of treatment, suspected GI bleeding, severe fatigue affecting daily life, during pregnancy.
dietAdvice: Iron-rich foods: red meat, liver, lentils, tofu, spinach, pumpkin seeds. Pair with vitamin C (lemon, orange). Avoid tea/coffee with meals.

description: Inflammation of the sinuses, usually caused by viral infection (most cases) or bacteria. Acute sinusitis lasts less than 4 weeks; chronic lasts over 12 weeks.
precautions: Nasal saline irrigation (neti pot) twice daily

medicines.name: Xylometazoline Nasal Spray (Otrivin)
medicines.warning: Do not use for more than 5 days — causes rebound congestion

medicines.name: Fluticasone Nasal Spray
medicines.childDose: 1 spray per nostril once daily (6+ yrs)

whenToSeeDoctor: High fever, severe headache, swelling around eyes, vision changes, stiff neck, symptoms lasting more than 10 days or worsening after initial improvement.
dietAdvice: Spicy foods and horseradish can help open sinuses. Stay very hydrated. Avoid dairy if mucus is a problem. Warm soups and broths are helpful.

symptoms: redness in the white of the eye
description: Inflammation of the conjunctiva (clear tissue covering the white of the eye). Can be viral, bacterial, or allergic. Infectious types are highly contagious.
precautions: Do not touch or rub eyes
Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup
Discard eye makeup used during infection
Children should stay home from school during infectious period

medicines.name: Chloramphenicol Eye Drops (for bacterial)
medicines.adultDose: 1-2 drops every 2 hours (reduce to 4x/day as improves)

medicines.name: Olopatadine Eye Drops (for allergic)
medicines.duration: As long as allergen exposure continues

whenToSeeDoctor: Severe eye pain, significant change in vision, sensitivity to light, corneal involvement, newborns with eye discharge, symptoms not improving after 5 days.
dietAdvice: Diet doesn't directly affect conjunctivitis recovery. Vitamin A rich foods (carrots, sweet potato) support general eye health.

description: Gastroesophageal reflux disease occurs when stomach acid repeatedly flows back into the food pipe. A very common digestive disorder affecting millions.
precautions: Do not eat 3 hours before bedtime
Elevate head of bed by 6-8 inches
Avoid trigger foods: citrus, tomatoes, chocolate, coffee, alcohol, fatty foods

medicines.name: Antacid (Gelusil/Digene)
medicines.adultDose: 10-20ml or 2 tablets after meals and at bedtime

whenToSeeDoctor: Difficulty swallowing, unexplained weight loss, vomiting blood, black stools, symptoms not responding to medication, symptoms lasting more than 4 weeks.
dietAdvice: Avoid spicy, oily, acidic foods. Eat slowly and chew well. Alkaline foods: bananas, oatmeal, ginger, green vegetables, melons. Avoid late-night eating.

description: A sleep disorder characterized by persistent difficulty with sleep initiation, maintenance, or quality. Can be short-term (acute) or long-term (chronic). Often secondary to anxiety, depression, or lifestyle factors.
precautions: Maintain consistent sleep schedule (same time daily)
Limit screen time 1 hour before bed
Keep bedroom cool, dark, and quiet
Exercise regularly but not near bedtime

medicines.name: Melatonin
medicines.duration: Short-term (2-4 weeks for jet lag or adjustment)

medicines.name: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl/Nytol)
medicines.warning: Only for short-term use. Can cause daytime grogginess.

medicines.name: Zolpidem (Ambien)
medicines.warning: Only take when you have 7-8 hours for sleep. Do not drive after taking.

whenToSeeDoctor: Insomnia lasting more than 3 months, daytime impairment affecting work/safety, associated with depression or anxiety, suspicion of sleep apnea (snoring, gasping).
dietAdvice: Avoid caffeine after 2 PM, limit alcohol, avoid heavy meals near bedtime. Sleep-promoting foods: warm milk, chamomile tea, banana, almonds, oatmeal.

symptoms: aching or stiffness along the spine
worsening pain with prolonged sitting or standing
description: One of the most common medical problems, affecting 8 in 10 people at some point. Most cases are mechanical (muscle strain, poor posture) and resolve within 4-6 weeks.
precautions: Stay active with gentle walking (bed rest worsens outcome)
Apply heat/ice for 15-20 minutes several times daily
Lift objects correctly (bend knees, straight back)
Core strengthening exercises once acute phase subsides

medicines.name: Ibuprofen
medicines.warning: Take with food. Avoid if history of peptic ulcer or kidney disease.

medicines.name: Diclofenac Gel (Topical)
medicines.adultDose: Apply 2-4g to affected area 3-4 times daily

medicines.name: Cyclobenzaprine (Muscle Relaxant)
medicines.warning: Causes drowsiness. Do not drive or operate machinery.

whenToSeeDoctor: Numbness or tingling in legs/feet, bowel or bladder problems, severe pain not improving with medication, pain following an injury, fever with back pain, pain in people over 50.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet: omega-3 fatty acids (fish, flaxseed), turmeric, ginger, cherries. Maintain healthy weight to reduce spinal load. Adequate vitamin D and calcium for bone health.

description: A widespread nutritional deficiency caused by insufficient sun exposure, dietary intake, or absorption. Affects bone health, immune function, and mental health.
precautions: Get 15-30 minutes of midday sun exposure daily (arms and legs exposed)
Take supplements if recommended by doctor
Regular vitamin D level tests (25-OH-D blood test)
Use vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) form for supplementation
Pair with vitamin K2 for bone absorption

medicines.name: Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3)
medicines.adultDose: 60,000 IU weekly for 8-12 weeks (loading), then 2000 IU daily
medicines.frequency: Weekly loading dose, then daily maintenance

whenToSeeDoctor: Level below 20 ng/mL on blood test, bone fractures with minimal trauma, chronic conditions (kidney/liver disease), malabsorption disorders, children with rickets signs.
dietAdvice: Vitamin D-rich foods: fatty fish (salmon, sardines), egg yolks, fortified milk, mushrooms (sun-exposed). Most people need supplements to reach optimal levels.

description: An infection that fills the air sacs in the lungs with fluid or pus, caused by bacteria, virus, or fungus. Can be life-threatening if severe or left untreated.
precautions: Complete full course of antibiotics even if better
whenToSeeDoctor: Shortness of breath at rest, SpO2 below 94%, confusion, high fever, chest pain, symptoms worsening despite antibiotics, signs of sepsis.
dietAdvice: High-protein foods for recovery, warm soups and broths, vitamin C-rich fruits, avoid cold foods. Honey is beneficial for cough suppression.

symptoms: mucus production (clear, white, yellowish, or bloody)
description: Inflammation of the airways in the lungs due to viral infection (acute) or chronic irritation. Acute bronchitis usually follows cold or flu.
precautions: Cover cough and wash hands regularly
whenToSeeDoctor: Cough lasting more than 3 weeks, coughing up blood, shortness of breath, chest pain, signs of pneumonia, high fever lasting over 1 week.
dietAdvice: Warm broths, honey and ginger tea, citrus fruits for vitamin C, avoid cold foods and carbonated beverages.

description: A serious infection causing inflammation of the membranes around the brain and spinal cord. A medical emergency requiring immediate hospitalization.
precautions: Get meningococcal vaccine (recommended for teenagers and young adults)
Practice good hygiene (especially important during outbreaks)
Avoid close contact with infected individuals
Do not delay treatment — early antibiotics are crucial

medicines.name: Vancomycin (IV)
medicines.type: Antibiotic (if penicillin allergy or resistant organisms)

whenToSeeDoctor: CALL EMERGENCY IMMEDIATELY for severe headache with fever and stiff neck. This is a medical emergency.
dietAdvice: Nutritional support is provided through IV in hospital; normal diet resuming gradually once patient stabilizes. Stay hydrated once able to eat.

description: A viral infection affecting the liver, transmitted through blood and body fluids. Can be acute or chronic. Preventable by vaccination.
precautions: Avoid contact with blood if you have cuts
Pregnant women should be tested and vaccinated
whenToSeeDoctor: Suspected exposure to hepatitis B, jaundice, dark urine, abdominal swelling, signs of liver failure, pregnant women with HBV.
dietAdvice: Adequate protein (not excessive), low salt diet, avoid alcohol completely. Balanced nutrition to support liver function. Regular monitoring of liver enzymes essential.

symptoms: pain radiating to shoulders and arms
description: Degenerative joint disease of the cervical spine (neck) causing wear and tear of cartilage and bone. Common with age and poor posture.
precautions: Maintain proper posture while sitting and working
Avoid heavy lifting and sudden jerky movements
whenToSeeDoctor: Severe pain, numbness affecting daily activities, signs of myelopathy (weak legs, difficulty walking), progressive neurological symptoms.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory foods: fatty fish, turmeric, ginger, berries. Calcium and vitamin D for bone health. Avoid processed and inflammatory foods.

description: A non-cancerous enlargement of the prostate gland common in older men, causing urinary symptoms. Does not increase cancer risk but affects quality of life.
precautions: Kegel exercises to strengthen pelvic muscles
Do not suppress urge to urinate

medicines.name: Finasteride (Proscar)
medicines.duration: Minimum 6 months needed to see effect

whenToSeeDoctor: Unable to pass urine, painful urination, blood in urine, signs of infection, PSA test abnormalities, symptoms severely affecting quality of life.
dietAdvice: Reduce caffeine and alcohol intake. Saw palmetto supplement may help. Adequate hydration during day, reduce fluids in evening. Zinc and lycopene-rich foods beneficial.

description: A neurological disorder characterized by recurrent seizures. Can vary in severity from brief absences to prolonged convulsions. Highly treatable with medications.
precautions: Avoid seizure triggers (sleep deprivation, stress, alcohol)
Do not drive until cleared by doctor
Inform close contacts about seizure management
Regular blood tests to monitor medication levels
whenToSeeDoctor: Status epilepticus (seizure lasting over 5 minutes), increased seizure frequency, medication side effects, difficulty controlling seizures despite medication.
dietAdvice: Avoid excessive alcohol, maintain regular sleep, consider ketogenic diet for drug-resistant cases, adequate hydration. Vitamin D supplementation often beneficial.

description: A chronic skin condition where pores become clogged with dead skin and sebum, allowing bacteria to multiply. Can range from mild to severe with significant scarring.
precautions: Wash face with gentle cleanser twice daily
Protect skin from sun (some medications increase sensitivity)

medicines.name: Benzoyl Peroxide
medicines.adultDose: Apply 2.5-10% twice daily to affected areas

whenToSeeDoctor: Severe nodular/cystic acne, signs of infection, significant scarring, acne not responding to OTC treatments, female with hormonal acne.
dietAdvice: Reduce dairy, refined sugars, and high-glycemic foods. Increase fruits, vegetables, and water intake. Avoid oily foods. Omega-3 fatty acids beneficial.

description: An allergic inflammation of the nasal membranes triggered by pollen, dust, or other allergens. Common seasonal disease affecting millions during spring and fall.
precautions: Keep windows closed during high pollen season
Shower and change clothes after being outdoors
Wash bedding weekly in hot water

medicines.name: Fluticasone Nasal Spray
medicines.childDose: 1 spray per nostril once daily (6+ yrs)
medicines.duration: During allergy season or long-term if chronic

whenToSeeDoctor: Symptoms not controlled with medications, signs of infection (yellow/green nasal discharge), symptoms affecting sleep and daily activities, suspicion of asthma.
dietAdvice: Avoid foods that cross-react with pollens (apples, nuts in birch pollen allergy). Local honey may provide some relief. Increase vitamin C intake.

description: A severe form of migraine preceded by sensory warnings (aura) like visual disturbances, typically lasting 20-60 minutes before headache onset.
precautions: Identify aura as warning sign to take medication early
Rest in dark, quiet room immediately upon aura onset
Avoid known triggers (hormones, foods, stress, weather changes)
Regular sleep and meal schedule critical

medicines.name: Sumatriptan (Imigran)
medicines.frequency: At aura onset, may repeat after 2 hours

whenToSeeDoctor: New aura pattern or characteristics changing, aura lasting more than 1 hour, weakness on one side of body, vision loss in one eye, suspected stroke symptoms.
dietAdvice: Avoid trigger foods like aged cheese, red wine, chocolate, processed meats. Maintain stable blood sugar with regular meals. Stay well hydrated.

symptoms: pain radiating to arms, jaw, or neck
description: A life-threatening condition where blood flow to heart muscle is blocked, causing tissue death. A medical emergency requiring immediate hospitalization.
precautions: CALL EMERGENCY IMMEDIATELY if chest pain occurs
Know your risk factors (age, smoking, family history)
Regular cardiac check-ups if high risk
Maintain healthy lifestyle with exercise and diet
whenToSeeDoctor: CALL EMERGENCY if chest pain, shortness of breath, severe symptoms develop. Always seek immediate medical care.
dietAdvice: Heart-healthy diet: fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins. Avoid saturated fats, trans fats, and high sodium foods.

symptoms: sudden weakness or numbness on one side
vision loss in one or both eyes
description: A blockage or rupture of blood vessels in the brain, causing sudden neurological deficits. A medical emergency where time is critical for treatment.
precautions: Remember FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency
Take medications for prevention if at risk

medicines.name: Alteplase (tPA)
medicines.type: Thrombolytic (clot-busting, must be given within 4.5 hours)

whenToSeeDoctor: CALL EMERGENCY IMMEDIATELY if FAST symptoms occur. Immediate hospital care is critical for best outcomes.
dietAdvice: Mediterranean diet recommended: olive oil, fish, vegetables, nuts. Limit sodium, saturated fats. Adequate fluids and fiber.

symptoms: usually no symptoms in early stages
description: A condition where bones become fragile and more susceptible to fractures due to low bone mineral density. More common in older adults and postmenopausal women.
precautions: Adequate calcium and vitamin D intake

medicines.name: Alendronate (Fosamx)
medicines.frequency: Once weekly on empty stomach, upright 30 min

whenToSeeDoctor: Unexpected fractures, severe back pain, height loss, DEXA scan showing low density, menopausal symptoms with bone concerns.
dietAdvice: Calcium-rich: dairy, leafy greens, tofu. Vitamin D: fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk. Adequate protein. Limit caffeine and salt.

description: A progressive lung disease characterized by airflow limitation, usually caused by smoking. Includes emphysema and chronic bronchitis.
precautions: Avoid air pollution and occupational exposures
Get flu and pneumonia vaccines annually
whenToSeeDoctor: Increased shortness of breath, worsening cough, fever, change in sputum color, chest pain, signs of exacerbation.
dietAdvice: High-protein foods for muscle maintenance, small frequent meals, adequate hydration. Antioxidant-rich foods. Avoid gas-producing foods.

description: Open sores that develop on the lining of the stomach, small intestine, or esophagus. Usually caused by H. pylori infection or NSAIDs.
precautions: Test and treat H. pylori infection
whenToSeeDoctor: Severe vomiting, black/bloody stools, signs of perforation (severe pain), persistent symptoms despite medication.
dietAdvice: Avoid spicy, oily, acidic foods. Eat small, frequent meals. Milk and yogurt soothe stomach. Avoid caffeine, alcohol, chocolate.

symptoms: severe pain in right upper abdomen
description: Inflammation of the gallbladder, often caused by gallstones blocking the bile duct. Can be acute or chronic.
precautions: Healthy diet low in fat and cholesterol
whenToSeeDoctor: Severe pain, fever, signs of pancreatitis, jaundice, symptoms lasting more than several hours, recurrent episodes.
dietAdvice: Low-fat diet, avoid fried foods, increase fiber gradually. Small frequent meals. Avoid dairy, fatty meats, eggs. Plenty of vegetables and whole grains.

description: A chronic inflammatory disorder of the gastrointestinal tract that can affect any part from mouth to anus. Requires long-term management.
whenToSeeDoctor: Severe diarrhea with blood, signs of dehydration, severe abdominal pain, fever, weight loss, symptoms not responding to treatment.
dietAdvice: Avoid high-fiber during flares, low-FODMAP diet, adequate protein, avoid trigger foods (varies per person). Stay hydrated. Nutritional supplements often needed.

description: An autoimmune disorder triggered by gluten consumption, causing intestinal inflammation and malabsorption. Managed by strict gluten-free diet.
precautions: Strict gluten-free diet (no wheat, barley, rye)
whenToSeeDoctor: Persistent symptoms despite gluten-free diet, continued weight loss, severe anemia, signs of osteoporosis, dermatitis worsening.
dietAdvice: Completely gluten-free diet is the only treatment. Focus on naturally gluten-free foods: fruits, vegetables, meat, fish, rice, potatoes, corn.

symptoms: symptoms 30 minutes to 2 hours after dairy
description: Inability to digest lactose (milk sugar) due to deficiency of lactase enzyme. Common in adults, especially non-European populations.
whenToSeeDoctor: Severe symptoms affecting quality of life, unexplained weight loss, signs of malnutrition, to rule out other causes of GI symptoms.
dietAdvice: Avoid milk, cream, ice cream, soft cheeses. Choose lactose-free milk, hard cheeses, yogurt with active cultures. Non-dairy sources: leafy greens, fortified plant milks.

description: A mental health disorder characterized by persistent, excessive worry that interferes with daily functioning. Highly treatable with therapy and/or medication.

medicines.name: Alprazolam (Xanax)
medicines.frequency: Up to 3 times daily as needed

whenToSeeDoctor: Anxiety interfering with daily activities, panic attacks, suicidal thoughts, symptoms lasting more than 6 months, difficulty with work/relationships.
dietAdvice: Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and sugary foods. Omega-3 rich foods (fish), whole grains, vegetables. Limit processed foods. Stay hydrated.

description: A serious mental health condition characterized by persistent depression lasting 2+ weeks affecting daily functioning. Highly treatable with counseling and/or medication.
precautions: Seek professional mental health care immediately

medicines.name: Sertraline (Zoloft)
medicines.duration: Minimum 4-6 weeks to assess, then continue

medicines.name: Bupropion (Wellbutrin)
medicines.frequency: Once daily (sustained release) or twice daily

whenToSeeDoctor: Suicidal thoughts, severe depression affecting functioning, inability to care for self, substance use, symptoms not improving with medication.
dietAdvice: Omega-3 rich foods, vitamin D sources, complex carbohydrates, lean proteins. Avoid excess sugar. Regular meals maintain blood sugar. Plenty of water.

symptoms: manic episodes: high energy, decreased sleep, racing thoughts, impulsive behavior
description: A serious mental health condition with alternating manic (elevated) and depressive episodes. Requires ongoing treatment and monitoring.
precautions: Identify early warning signs of episodes
whenToSeeDoctor: Signs of manic or depressive episode, suicidal/homicidal thoughts, medication side effects, inability to manage symptoms, substance use.
dietAdvice: Regular meals for blood sugar stability, adequate sleep support, avoid alcohol and caffeine. Omega-3 fatty acids beneficial. Consistent routine important.

symptoms: repetitive behaviors (compulsions) done to relieve anxiety
time spent on compulsions affecting daily life
description: A mental health condition with obsessive thoughts and compulsive behaviors that interfere with daily life. Responds well to specific therapies.
precautions: Cognitive behavioral therapy (particularly ERP — Exposure and Response Prevention)
whenToSeeDoctor: Compulsions consuming 1+ hour daily, severe anxiety or distress, inability to resist compulsions, interference with work/school/relationships.
dietAdvice: Adequate nutrition for brain health, omega-3 foods, B vitamins, antioxidants. Avoid excess caffeine. Regular meal times stabilize mood.

description: A mental health condition developing after exposure to traumatic events like accidents, assault, or war. Highly treatable with appropriate therapy.
whenToSeeDoctor: Intrusive trauma memories, nightmares affecting sleep, suicidal thoughts, substance abuse as coping mechanism, severe anxiety.
dietAdvice: Regular meals stabilize mood, omega-3 rich foods support brain health, complex carbohydrates help serotonin, limit caffeine and alcohol.

description: A neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. Highly treatable with medication and behavioral strategies.

medicines.name: Methylphenidate (Ritalin/Concerta)
medicines.frequency: Once daily (extended release) or twice daily

whenToSeeDoctor: Symptoms affecting school or work, behavioral concerns, medication side effects, growth concerns in children, comorbid anxiety or mood disorders.
dietAdvice: Adequate protein at breakfast, whole grains, omega-3 foods, limit sugar and artificial additives. Regular meal timing. Plenty of water.

description: A neurodevelopmental condition with varying presentations affecting social communication and behavior. Not curable but highly manageable with early intervention.
whenToSeeDoctor: Behavioral escalation, medication side effects, difficulty with school/work, safety concerns, mental health comorbidities.
dietAdvice: Nutritious whole foods, identify food sensitivities, adequate protein and healthy fats. Avoid excess sugar. Multivitamin supplementation often helpful.

symptoms: negative symptoms (flat affect, lack of motivation)
description: A serious mental health condition with psychotic symptoms. Requires long-term medication management and supportive services.
whenToSeeDoctor: Psychotic symptoms, medication non-adherence, worsening hallucinations/delusions, suicidal/homicidal thoughts, side effect management.
dietAdvice: Adequate nutrition for medication metabolism, regular meals, limit caffeine (can worsen symptoms), omega-3 foods, avoid alcohol completely.

description: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder affecting movement control due to dopamine deficiency. Symptoms worsen gradually over time.
whenToSeeDoctor: Worsening motor symptoms, medication wearing off, dyskinesia (involuntary movements), non-motor symptoms (mood, cognition), fall risk assessment.
dietAdvice: Adequate protein (take with levodopa), high-fiber diet for constipation, regular meals, stay hydrated. Vitamin D and calcium for bone health.

description: A progressive neurodegenerative disease causing dementia, characterized by amyloid and tau protein accumulation in the brain.
whenToSeeDoctor: Memory problems affecting daily life, behavioral changes, caregiver concerns, medication effectiveness assessment, safety evaluation.
dietAdvice: Mediterranean diet with emphasis on vegetables, fish, nuts, olive oil. Adequate vitamin D and B vitamins. Limit processed foods and sugar.

description: An autoimmune disease affecting the central nervous system, with unpredictable attacks causing inflammation and demyelination.
whenToSeeDoctor: New neurological symptoms, vision changes, balance problems, cognitive decline, medication side effects, infection signs.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet, omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin D supplementation critical, adequate vitamin B12, calcium for bone health.

description: An autoimmune disease affecting multiple organ systems. More common in women, with variable severity and manifestations.

medicines.name: Prednisone
medicines.duration: Taper gradually or maintain at low dose

whenToSeeDoctor: New rash or photosensitivity, joint swelling/pain, chest pain, kidney symptoms (blood/protein in urine), constitutional symptoms worsening.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet, omega-3 rich foods, avoid alfalfa sprouts (can flare disease), adequate vitamin D, antioxidant foods, limit sun-exposed foods.

description: A chronic pain syndrome characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain and fatigue. Diagnosis requires ruling out other conditions.
whenToSeeDoctor: Severe pain affecting daily activities, depression or anxiety, sleep disorders, need for diagnostic confirmation, medication adjustments.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory foods, adequate protein, complex carbohydrates, omega-3 fatty acids. Avoid trigger foods, stay hydrated, limit caffeine.

description: An autoimmune connective tissue disease causing excessive collagen deposition in skin and organs. Limited and diffuse forms exist.
whenToSeeDoctor: New or worsening skin changes, shortness of breath, kidney involvement (protein/blood in urine), severe Raynaud's episodes.
dietAdvice: Soft foods if swallowing difficult, adequate protein and calories, anti-inflammatory foods, avoid foods that trigger reflux, stay hydrated.

description: An autoimmune thyroid disease where antibodies attack thyroid tissue, causing gradual thyroid failure. Most common cause of hypothyroidism.
whenToSeeDoctor: TSH changes, persistent fatigue despite medication, other autoimmune conditions developing, pregnancy, medication non-response.
dietAdvice: Adequate iodine (iodized salt, seafood), selenium-rich foods (Brazil nuts), minimize goitrogens, gluten-free if celiac present, antioxidant foods.

description: An autoimmune thyroid disease causing thyroid overactivity due to stimulating antibodies. Most common cause of hyperthyroidism.
whenToSeeDoctor: New cardiac symptoms, worsening eye symptoms, signs of thyroid storm, agranulocytosis symptoms (sore throat, fever), medication allergies.
dietAdvice: Avoid iodine-rich foods initially (seafood, seaweed, iodized salt), increase calories to prevent weight loss, anti-inflammatory foods, adequate protein.

description: A cluster of conditions (abdominal obesity, hypertension, elevated glucose, dyslipidemia) increasing cardiovascular disease and diabetes risk.
whenToSeeDoctor: New diagnosis of metabolic syndrome, progression of components, cardiovascular symptoms, diabetes development, medication adjustments.
dietAdvice: DASH diet, reduce refined carbohydrates and added sugars, increase whole grains and vegetables, limit saturated fats, adequate fiber, control portions.

description: Gradual loss of kidney function over months to years. Caused by diabetes, hypertension, or other diseases. Requires careful management.
precautions: Fluid restriction as kidney function declines
whenToSeeDoctor: Elevated creatinine, reduced GFR, proteinuria, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, need for dialysis planning, transplant evaluation.
dietAdvice: Restrict sodium (2g/day), potassium (if advanced), phosphorus (if advanced), protein (based on stage), adequate calories. Follow renal dietitian advice.

description: Autoimmune skin condition causing inflamed, scaly patches.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory foods, avoid alcohol

description: Chronic inflammatory skin condition with itching and rashes.
dietAdvice: Identify food triggers, maintain hydration

description: Allergic inflammation of nasal passages causing sneezing and congestion.
dietAdvice: Avoid trigger foods

description: Inflammation of airways producing cough and mucus.
whenToSeeDoctor: Symptoms lasting >3 weeks, blood in sputum
dietAdvice: Warm liquids, honey, avoid irritants

description: Serious lung infection causing inflammation and fluid accumulation.
dietAdvice: Nutritious foods, plenty of fluids

description: Inflammation of the pancreas causing severe pain and digestive issues.
dietAdvice: Low fat, avoid alcohol, small meals

description: Autoimmune disorder triggered by gluten consumption affecting intestines.
dietAdvice: Complete gluten elimination

description: Chronic inflammatory bowel disease causing intestinal inflammation.
dietAdvice: Identify trigger foods, maintain nutrition

description: Chronic inflammation of colon and rectum with ulcer formation.
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory diet, avoid triggers

description: Viral liver infection usually spread through contaminated food/water.
dietAdvice: Light foods, avoid alcohol and fatty foods

description: Viral liver disease spread through blood and body fluids.
dietAdvice: Avoid alcohol, balanced nutrition

description: Blood-borne viral infection of the liver with potential for chronic disease.
dietAdvice: Liver-friendly diet, no alcohol

description: Advanced liver disease with severe scarring and organ failure risk.
dietAdvice: Low sodium, adequate protein, no alcohol

description: Mineral deposits forming in kidneys causing severe pain when passed.
whenToSeeDoctor: Severe pain, fever with pain, no stone passage
dietAdvice: High water intake, low sodium, limit calcium oxalate

description: Eye disease with elevated intraocular pressure damaging optic nerve.
whenToSeeDoctor: Eye pain with vision loss, requires immediate evaluation
dietAdvice: Anti-inflammatory foods, antioxidants

description: Clouding of eye lens impairing vision, usually age-related.
whenToSeeDoctor: Significant vision impairment affecting daily activities
dietAdvice: Antioxidant-rich foods, vitamin C and E

description: Insufficient tear production or quality causing eye discomfort.
dietAdvice: Omega-3 foods, stay hydrated

description: Inflammation of sinus cavities causing congestion and facial pain.
dietAdvice: Warm fluids, honey, avoid dairy products

description: Infection of middle ear causing pain, common in children.
dietAdvice: Warm compresses, avoid irritants

description: Inner ear disorder causing severe vertigo and hearing problems.
dietAdvice: Low sodium, caffeine restriction, adequate hydration

description: Vertigo is a medical condition requiring professional diagnosis and treatment.
dietAdvice: Consult with healthcare provider for dietary recommendations.
